chapters.title: Biology — The Invisible Living World — the Cell

skills.name: States of matter: solid, liquid, gas

questions.stem: What are the basic building blocks of chalk called?
questions.explain: Constituent particles are the smallest units that make up chalk.
questions.evidence: These units are called constituent particles of chalk.

questions.stem: What happens to sugar when it dissolves in water?
options: It changes into a new substance
It breaks into its constituent particles
questions.explain: Sugar breaks into smaller particles that mix with water.
questions.evidence: When sugar dissolves in water, it breaks up into its constituent particles which cannot be broken down further.

questions.stem: Why do solids have a definite shape?
options: Because their particles are loosely packed
Because their particles are tightly packed
Because they have no interparticle forces
Because they are made of large particles
questions.explain: Strong forces keep solid particles in fixed positions.
questions.evidence: In solids, the particles are tightly packed and the interparticle attractions are very strong.

questions.stem: How do liquids differ from solids in terms of shape?
options: Liquids take the shape of their container
questions.explain: Liquids flow and adapt to the shape of their container.
questions.evidence: Liquids do not have a fixed shape and take the shape of the container they are kept in.

questions.stem: What happens to the interparticle forces when a solid is heated?
questions.explain: Heating makes particles vibrate more, weakening the forces.
questions.evidence: The interparticle forces of attraction get weakened and the solid gets converted into the liquid state.

questions.stem: Which of the following states of matter has the weakest interparticle attractions?
questions.explain: Gas particles move freely with very little attraction between them.
questions.evidence: In gases, the interparticle attractions are negligible.

questions.stem: What is the boiling point of water?
questions.explain: Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius.
questions.evidence: Recall Curiosity, Grade 6 chapter ‘Temperature and Its Measurement’, where you observed the temperature of boiling water (liquid).

questions.stem: How do gases behave in a container?
options: They fill the entire available space
questions.explain: Gases spread out to fill any container they are in.
questions.evidence: The smoke fills the entire space in the Gas Jar B, indicating that gases do not have a fixed volume and tend to occupy the entire available space.

questions.stem: What happens to the particles of a liquid when it is heated to its boiling point?
options: They move apart and form a gas
questions.explain: Heating causes particles to move faster and spread out into a gas.
questions.evidence: The movement of particles becomes so vigorous that they move apart from each other, resulting in a decrease in the interparticle forces of attraction.

questions.stem: What is the process called when a liquid turns into a gas at temperatures below its boiling point?
questions.explain: Evaporation is when a liquid slowly turns into gas at lower temperatures.
questions.evidence: This slower process is known as evaporation — about which you have learnt in earlier grades.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true about the particles in a solid?
options: They can move freely past each other
They are in fixed positions and can only vibrate
They are far apart from each other
questions.explain: Solid particles are tightly packed and can only vibrate in place.
questions.evidence: The particles can only move to and fro about their positions (vibrate or oscillate) but cannot move past each other.

questions.stem: What determines the physical state of a substance?
questions.explain: The stronger the attractions, the more solid the state.
questions.evidence: The strength of these forces ultimately decides the physical state of the substances.

questions.stem: Why do gases not have a fixed shape or volume?
options: Because their particles are tightly packed
Because their particles are loosely packed
Because their particles are free to move in all directions
Because they are made of large particles
questions.explain: Gas particles move freely, so they spread out and fill any space.
questions.evidence: The particles in gases move freely in all directions and the interparticle attractions are negligible.

questions.stem: What is the smallest unit that makes up a larger piece of a substance or material called?
questions.explain: Constituent particles are the building blocks of matter.
questions.evidence: A constituent particle is the basic unit that makes up a larger piece of a substance or material.

questions.stem: What happens to the interparticle forces when a liquid is heated to its boiling point?
questions.explain: Heating makes particles move faster, weakening the forces.
questions.evidence: The movement of particles becomes so vigorous that they move apart from each other, resulting in a decrease in the interparticle forces of attraction.

questions.stem: What is the process called when a solid turns into a liquid?
questions.explain: Melting is when a solid becomes a liquid.
questions.evidence: The temperature at which this happens is the melting point of the solid.

questions.stem: What is the term for the temperature at which a liquid turns into a gas?
questions.explain: The boiling point is when a liquid becomes a gas.
questions.evidence: The temperature at which a liquid boils and turns into vapour at atmospheric pressure is called its boiling point.

questions.stem: What is the main reason gases can fill any container they are in?
questions.explain: Weak attractions allow gas particles to move freely and fill space.
questions.evidence: The particles in gases move freely in all directions and the interparticle attractions are negligible.

questions.stem: Which state of matter has particles that can only vibrate in place?
questions.explain: Solid particles are tightly packed and can only vibrate.
questions.evidence: The particles can only move to and fro about their positions (vibrate or oscillate) but cannot move past each other.

questions.stem: What is the process called when a liquid turns into a gas at its boiling point?
questions.explain: Boiling is when a liquid rapidly turns into gas at its boiling point.
questions.evidence: At the boiling point, the formation of vapour is very fast and occurs not only at the surface but also within the liquid.

questions.stem: What is the main characteristic of gases that distinguishes them from solids and liquids?
options: They have no fixed shape or volume
questions.explain: Gases spread out to fill any container, unlike solids and liquids.
questions.evidence: Gases do not have a fixed shape or volume and tend to occupy the entire available space.

questions.stem: What is the primary function of the cell membrane?
options: To enclose the cytoplasm and nucleus
questions.explain: The cell membrane's main role is to enclose and protect the cell's contents.
questions.evidence: The cell membrane encloses the cytoplasm and nucleus.

questions.stem: Which organelle is responsible for photosynthesis in plant cells?
questions.explain: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, which is essential for photosynthesis.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis.

questions.stem: What is the role of the vacuole in plant cells?
options: To store important substances and maintain cell shape
To separate one cell from another
questions.explain: Vacuoles store substances and help maintain the cell's shape.
questions.evidence: This helps the plant cell store important substances, get rid of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.stem: How do animal cells differ from plant cells in terms of vacuoles?
options: Animal cells have large vacuoles, while plant cells do not
Animal cells have small vacuoles, if present, while plant cells have large vacuoles
Animal cells have no vacuoles, while plant cells have small vacuoles
Animal cells and plant cells have vacuoles of the same size
questions.explain: Animal cells typically have smaller vacuoles compared to plant cells.
questions.evidence: In animal cells, vacuoles are usually not present, if present, they are usually small.

questions.stem: What structure provides rigidity and strength to plant cells?
questions.explain: The cell wall is a rigid structure that supports plant cells.
questions.evidence: The cell wall in the plant cell provides rigidity and strength to plants.

questions.stem: Which part of the cell is responsible for regulating all activities within the cell?
questions.explain: The nucleus controls the cell's activities and growth.
questions.evidence: The nucleus regulates all activities that occur within the cell.

questions.stem: What is the main difference in shape between muscle cells and nerve cells?
options: Muscle cells are round, nerve cells are spindle-shaped
Muscle cells are spindle-shaped, nerve cells are long with branches
Muscle cells are long with branches, nerve cells are round
Muscle cells and nerve cells have the same shape
questions.explain: Muscle cells are spindle-shaped, while nerve cells are long and branched.
questions.evidence: A muscle cell (Fig. 2.6a) is shaped like a spindle, while a nerve cell (Fig. 2.6b) is very long and has branches.

questions.stem: What is the function of cytoplasm in a cell?
options: To contain other components of the cell and compounds
questions.explain: Cytoplasm holds the cell's components and is where many life processes occur.
questions.evidence: Cytoplasm contains other components of the cell and compounds, such as carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and mineral salts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a basic part of a cell?
questions.explain: Chloroplasts are not found in all cells, only in plant cells for photosynthesis.
questions.evidence: These three — cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus — are the basic parts of a cell.

questions.stem: Why do plant cells have a cell wall, but animal cells do not?
questions.explain: The cell wall gives plant cells their firm structure, which is not needed in animal cells.
questions.evidence: The cell wall in the plant cell provides rigidity and strength to plants.

questions.stem: What is the primary function of plastids in plant cells?
options: To perform photosynthesis and store substances
questions.explain: Plastids are involved in photosynthesis and storage in plant cells.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis. In non-green parts, they help in the storage of substances.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures is porous and allows the entry and exit of materials?
questions.explain: The cell membrane controls what enters and leaves the cell.
questions.evidence: It is porous and allows the entry of materials essential for life processes and the exit of waste material.

questions.stem: Which organelle is typically larger in plant cells compared to animal cells?
questions.explain: Plant cells have larger vacuoles to store substances and maintain shape.
questions.evidence: In animal cells, vacuoles are usually not present, if present, they are usually small.

questions.stem: What is the role of chlorophyll in chloroplasts?
questions.explain: Chlorophyll is essential for capturing light energy for photosynthesis.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis.

questions.stem: Which part of the cell is responsible for separating one cell from another?
questions.explain: The cell membrane acts as a boundary between cells.
questions.evidence: The cell membrane separates one cell from another.

questions.stem: How do the shapes of muscle and nerve cells relate to their functions?
options: Muscle cells are long for contraction, nerve cells are round for signal transmission
Muscle cells are spindle-shaped for contraction, nerve cells are long for signal transmission
Muscle cells are round for contraction, nerve cells are spindle-shaped for signal transmission
Muscle cells and nerve cells have the same shape for different functions
questions.explain: The shape of each cell type is suited to its specific function.
questions.evidence: A muscle cell (Fig. 2.6a) is shaped like a spindle, while a nerve cell (Fig. 2.6b) is very long and has branches.

questions.stem: What is the main function of the cell wall in plant cells?
questions.explain: The cell wall supports and protects plant cells.
questions.evidence: The cell wall in the plant cell provides rigidity and strength to plants.

questions.stem: Which structure is found in plant cells but not typically in animal cells?
questions.explain: Chloroplasts are unique to plant cells for photosynthesis.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis.

questions.stem: What is the primary role of the nucleus in a cell?
options: To regulate all activities within the cell
questions.explain: The nucleus is the control center of the cell.
questions.evidence: The nucleus regulates all activities that occur within the cell.

questions.stem: How do plant cells maintain their shape?
options: Through the vacuole and cell wall
questions.explain: The vacuole and cell wall help maintain the plant cell's shape.
questions.evidence: This helps the plant cell store important substances, get rid of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.stem: What is the primary function of the cytoplasm?
options: To contain other components of the cell and compounds
questions.explain: Cytoplasm is where most cellular processes occur.
questions.evidence: Cytoplasm contains other components of the cell and compounds, such as carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and mineral salts.

questions.stem: Which organelle is responsible for storing substances in plant cells?
questions.explain: Vacuoles store substances and help maintain cell shape.
questions.evidence: This helps the plant cell store important substances, get rid of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.stem: What is the difference in vacuole size between plant and animal cells?
options: Plant cells have smaller vacuoles than animal cells
Plant cells have larger vacuoles than animal cells
Both have the same size vacuoles
questions.explain: Plant cells have larger vacuoles to store more substances.
questions.evidence: In animal cells, vacuoles are usually not present, if present, they are usually small.

questions.stem: Which structure is responsible for photosynthesis in plant cells?
questions.explain: Chloroplasts are essential for photosynthesis in plant cells.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis.

questions.stem: What is the role of the cell membrane in a cell?
options: To enclose the cytoplasm and nucleus
questions.explain: The cell membrane protects and contains the cell's contents.
questions.evidence: The cell membrane encloses the cytoplasm and nucleus.

questions.stem: What is a concave mirror?
options: A mirror with a reflecting surface that curves inwards
A mirror with a reflecting surface that curves outwards
questions.explain: A concave mirror curves inward, like the inside of a spoon.
questions.evidence: A spherical mirror, which has a reflecting surface that curves inwards, is called a concave mirror.

questions.stem: Which type of mirror is used as a side-view mirror in vehicles?
questions.explain: Convex mirrors provide a wider field of view, which is useful for side-view mirrors.
questions.evidence: Look at the side-view mirrors on vehicles. These mirrors are convex.

questions.stem: What happens to the image when an object is moved away from a concave mirror?
options: The image becomes erect and enlarged
The image becomes inverted and enlarged
The image becomes inverted and smaller
questions.explain: As the object moves away, the image inverts and reduces in size.
questions.evidence: However, when the object is moved farther away, the image becomes inverted. Initially, the image is enlarged in size and then keeps getting smaller.

questions.stem: What is the angle of reflection when a light ray falls along the normal to a mirror?
questions.explain: When light falls along the normal, it reflects back on the same path, making the angle of reflection 0°.
questions.evidence: Finally, let the incident beam fall on the mirror along the normal and observe the direction of the reflected beam. What would be the angle of incidence and angle of reflection in this case? Both the angles would be zero in this case.

questions.stem: Which of the following is true for a convex lens?
options: It does not affect light beams
questions.explain: A convex lens focuses light beams to a point, hence it converges them.
questions.evidence: The convex lens converges the light falling on it while the concave lens diverges the light.

questions.stem: What type of image does a convex mirror always form?
questions.explain: Convex mirrors always produce smaller, upright images.
questions.evidence: In case of a convex mirror, the image is always erect and smaller than the object, that is diminished.

questions.stem: Why are convex mirrors used in road safety mirrors?
options: They provide a larger view area
They provide a smaller view area
questions.explain: Convex mirrors give a wider field of view, which helps in seeing more of the road.
questions.evidence: Further, such convex mirrors are installed at road intersections or sharp bends to provide drivers from both sides the visibility of the other side and prevent collisions.

questions.stem: What happens to parallel light beams when they fall on a concave mirror?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors focus parallel beams to a point, causing them to converge.
questions.evidence: However, when multiple beams of light fall upon a concave mirror, the multiple reflected beams get closer, that is, they converge.

questions.stem: What is the main function of a convex lens in optical devices?
questions.explain: Convex lenses focus light to a point, which is useful in devices like cameras and glasses.
questions.evidence: A convex lens is also called a converging lens while a concave lens is called a diverging lens.

questions.stem: Which statement is true about the image formed by a concave lens?
options: It is always erect and enlarged
It is always erect and diminished
It is always inverted and enlarged
It is always inverted and diminished
questions.explain: Concave lenses always produce smaller, upright images.
questions.evidence: An object placed behind a concave lens and seen through the lens, always appears erect and diminished in size.

questions.stem: What happens to the image when an object is placed close to a concave mirror?
options: The image is erect and enlarged
The image is inverted and enlarged
The image is erect and diminished
The image is inverted and diminished
questions.explain: Close objects in concave mirrors appear larger and upright.
questions.evidence: In the concave mirror, when the object is placed close to the mirror, the image is erect but larger than the object in size, that is, enlarged.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a characteristic of a plane mirror?
options: It forms an erect image of the same size
questions.explain: Plane mirrors reflect images that are the same size and orientation as the object.
questions.evidence: A plane mirror always forms an erect image of the same size as the object.

questions.stem: What is the effect of a convex lens on parallel light beams?
questions.explain: Convex lenses focus parallel beams to a point, converging them.
questions.evidence: The convex lens converges the light falling on it while the concave lens diverges the light.

questions.stem: Which type of lens is used in a magnifying glass?
questions.explain: Magnifying glasses use convex lenses to enlarge images.
questions.evidence: Have you seen a magnifying glass as shown in Fig. 10.14? It is also a lens that helps in reading small print by making the letters appear bigger.

questions.stem: What is the law of reflection regarding the angle of incidence and reflection?
options: The angle of incidence is always greater than the angle of reflection
The angle of incidence is always less than the angle of reflection
The angle of incidence is equal to the angle of reflection
The angle of incidence is unrelated to the angle of reflection
questions.explain: The law states that the angle at which light hits a surface equals the angle at which it reflects.
questions.evidence: If done carefully, the experiment shows that the angle of incidence (i) is equal to the angle of reflection (r). This is a law of reflection.

questions.stem: Which type of mirror is used by dentists to provide an enlarged view of teeth?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors enlarge images, useful for detailed views like in dentistry.
questions.evidence: It is a concave mirror which provides an enlarged view of teeth when held close to the teeth inside the mouth.

questions.stem: What is the shape of a convex lens?
options: Thicker at the edges than the middle
Thicker at the middle than the edges
questions.explain: Convex lenses bulge outwards, being thicker in the center.
questions.evidence: A lens which is thicker at the middle as compared to the edges is called a convex lens.

questions.stem: Which type of mirror is used in the headlights of cars?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors focus light into a beam, useful for headlights.
questions.evidence: The reflectors of torches, headlights of cars and scooters are concave in shape.

questions.stem: Which of the following is a characteristic of a concave lens?
questions.explain: Concave lenses spread out light beams, causing them to diverge.
questions.evidence: The convex lens converges the light falling on it while the concave lens diverges the light.

questions.stem: What is the effect of a concave mirror on parallel light beams?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors focus parallel beams to a point, causing them to converge.
questions.evidence: However, when multiple beams of light fall upon a concave mirror, the multiple reflected beams get closer, that is, they converge.

questions.stem: What type of image does a concave mirror form when the object is far away?
questions.explain: Far objects in concave mirrors appear smaller and inverted.
questions.evidence: However, when the object is moved farther away, the image becomes inverted. Initially, the image is enlarged in size and then keeps getting smaller.

questions.stem: Which type of lens is thicker at the edges than at the middle?
questions.explain: Concave lenses are thinner in the center, causing them to spread light.
questions.evidence: A lens which is thicker at the edges as compared to the middle is called a concave lens.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of using a concave mirror in a solar cooker?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors focus sunlight to a point, increasing heat for cooking.
questions.evidence: Devices which concentrate sunlight into a small area, using mirrors and lenses, are called solar concentrators.

questions.stem: What is the effect of a convex lens on sunlight?
questions.explain: Convex lenses focus sunlight to a point, concentrating heat.
questions.evidence: Repeat Activity 10.7 by putting a convex lens in the path of sunrays in place of a concave mirror. Could you burn the paper?

questions.stem: Which type of mirror is used in telescopes to gather light?
questions.explain: Concave mirrors focus light, making them ideal for telescopes.
questions.evidence: Most modern telescopes are reflecting telescope that use curved mirrors, with the main mirror being a large concave mirror.

questions.stem: Who first introduced the concept of Parmanu, which refers to an indivisible particle?
questions.explain: Acharya Kanad is credited with the concept of Parmanu in ancient Indian philosophy.
questions.evidence: Acharya Kanad, an ancient Indian philosopher, first spoke about the idea of a Parmanu (atom).

questions.stem: What is the name of the work where Acharya Kanad discussed the idea of Parmanu?
questions.explain: Vaisheshika Sutras is the work where Acharya Kanad discussed Parmanu.
questions.evidence: This idea was written in his work called Vaisheshika Sutras.

questions.stem: What concept did Acharya Kanad introduce that is similar to the modern concept of an atom?
questions.explain: Parmanu is akin to the modern concept of an atom.
questions.evidence: Acharya Kanad, an ancient Indian philosopher, first spoke about the idea of a Parmanu (atom).

questions.stem: What is the primary subject of the Vaisheshika Sutras?
questions.explain: Vaisheshika Sutras discusses the concept of Parmanu.
questions.evidence: This idea was written in his work called Vaisheshika Sutras.

questions.stem: What is the function of chloroplasts in plant cells?
options: They contain chlorophyll and help in photosynthesis.
They provide structural support to the cell.
They are involved in cell division.
questions.explain: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll, which is essential for photosynthesis.
questions.evidence: Some plastids, like chloroplasts, contain chlorophyll, which makes them green and helps in photosynthesis.

questions.stem: What is the primary role of vacuoles in plant cells?
options: To store important substances and maintain cell shape.
To produce energy for the cell.
To transport nutrients across the cell membrane.
questions.explain: Vacuoles store substances and help maintain the shape of plant cells.
questions.evidence: This helps the plant cell store important substances, get rid of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.stem: Which structure is typically absent or small in animal cells compared to plant cells?
questions.explain: Animal cells either lack vacuoles or have very small ones compared to plant cells.
questions.evidence: In animal cells, vacuoles are usually not present, if present, they are usually small.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of plastids in non-green parts of plants?
options: To help in the storage of substances.
questions.explain: Plastids in non-green parts are involved in storage.
questions.evidence: In non-green parts, they help in the storage of substances.

questions.stem: What is the main function of the large vacuole in plant cells?
options: To store important substances and maintain cell shape.
questions.explain: The vacuole stores substances and maintains cell shape.
questions.evidence: This helps the plant cell store important substances, get rid of waste, and maintain the shape of the cell.

questions.stem: What is the role of vacuoles in animal cells, if present?
options: To store certain substances dissolved in water.
questions.explain: In animal cells, vacuoles store substances dissolved in water.
questions.evidence: These small vacuoles store certain substances dissolved in water.

questions.stem: What is the process used to create spherical mirrors?
options: Grinding and polishing a flat glass piece
questions.explain: Spherical mirrors are made by grinding and polishing flat glass, not by slicing spheres.
questions.evidence: Instead, they are created by grinding and polishing a flat glass piece into a curved surface.

questions.stem: Where is the reflective coating applied to form a concave mirror?
questions.explain: The coating on the outer surface creates a concave mirror.
questions.evidence: If a reflective coating (like a thin layer of aluminium) is applied on the outer curved surface, it forms a concave mirror.

questions.stem: What type of mirror is used in most modern telescopes?
questions.explain: Modern telescopes use concave mirrors as their main mirror.
questions.evidence: Most modern telescopes are reflecting telescope that use curved mirrors, with the main mirror being a large concave mirror.

questions.stem: What type of mirrors are used as side-view mirrors on vehicles?
questions.explain: Convex mirrors are used in vehicles for a wider field of view.
questions.evidence: Look at the side-view mirrors on vehicles (Fig. 10.7a). These mirrors are convex.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of solar concentrators?
questions.explain: Solar concentrators focus sunlight to produce heat or electricity.
questions.evidence: Devices which concentrate sunlight into a small area, using mirrors and lenses, are called solar concentrators.

questions.stem: What ancient method did astronomers use to observe stars and planets over 800 years ago?
questions.explain: Astronomers used water bowls to observe celestial bodies.
questions.evidence: More than 800 years ago, during the time of the great Indian mathematician Bhāskara II, astronomers used shallow bowls of water to observe the stars and planets.

questions.stem: What is the main function of a convex mirror in road safety?
questions.explain: Convex mirrors provide an erect and smaller image for better visibility.
questions.evidence: They always form an erect image of the traffic behind and smaller than the actual vehicles.

questions.stem: What is the role of the normal in the reflection of light?
options: It divides the incident and reflected rays
It changes the color of light
questions.explain: The normal helps define the plane of reflection.
questions.evidence: However, the direction of the reflected ray is such that the incident ray, the normal at the point of incidence, and the reflected ray all lie in the same plane in both cases.

questions.stem: What is a key feature of the reflected ray in relation to the incident ray and normal?
options: They lie in the same plane
questions.explain: The incident ray, normal, and reflected ray lie in the same plane.
questions.evidence: ...the incident ray, the normal at the point of incidence, and the reflected ray all lie in the same plane in both cases.

questions.stem: What is one use of solar furnaces mentioned in the text?
questions.explain: Solar furnaces can reach high temperatures to melt steel.
questions.evidence: Solar furnaces are even used for melting steel!

questions.stem: What did ancient astronomers use to measure the positions of stars and planets?
questions.explain: They used water bowls to reflect and measure celestial positions.
questions.evidence: ...astronomers used shallow bowls of water to observe the stars and planets.

questions.stem: What is the main characteristic of the image formed by convex mirrors used in vehicles?
questions.explain: Convex mirrors form smaller, erect images for better rear visibility.
questions.evidence: They always form an erect image of the traffic behind and smaller than the actual vehicles.

questions.stem: Robert Hooke described cells in a book he published. What was it called?
questions.explain: The chapter shows Hooke's book and his microscope — the book is Micrographia.
questions.evidence: Fig. 2.2: (a) Micrographia book; (b) Microscope of Robert Hooke;

questions.stem: In the schematic drawings of cells, which one is the ANIMAL cell — the one with no cell wall, no chloroplast and only a tiny vacuole?
questions.explain: An animal cell lacks the cell wall, chloroplasts and the large vacuole a plant cell has.
questions.evidence: A schematic drawing of (a) An animal cell and (b) A plant

questions.stem: Acharya Kanad believed matter is made of tiny particles that cannot be divided further. What did he call them?
questions.explain: Kanad called the indivisible, eternal particle a Parmanu — the ancient Indian idea of the atom.
questions.evidence: He believed that matter is made up of tiny, indivisible eternal particles called Parmanu.
